title: UNIT 2 OEDIPUS REX: SUMMARY AND ANALYSIS

text:
2.0 OBJECTIVES

After we go through the Unit, we will have learnt about the followingः brief summary of the play in 5 sections, to analyse the play mnou the tragic collapse of Oedipus and its justification, and the role of the Chorus in the play 2.1 INTRODUCTION The play is not a lengthy play as it comprises around 1६८४ lines. We will give OPLES you a brief summary of the play in five sections something that corresponds to the current five scenes or Five Act structure in a play Aristotle was the first to insist on १ division of a play into Acts and Scenes so that the plot RSITY is orderly arranged into sequential parts. But in ancient Greek theatre, no such division existed and the plot flowed through the lines in an orderly structured way In the last Unit, we had learnt how in a Tragedy events are sequenced sO that they give shape to the terrible events that helps us to unravel the cause of the ghastly event. Let us begin by summarising and analysing the plot of the Oedipus Rex.
title: 2.0 OBJECTIVES

text:
2.2 SUMMARY AND ANALYSIS OF OEDIPUS REX: SECTION 1: LINES ] 337 The play begins with Oedipus meeting a procession of priests and citizens of his kingdom in Thebes. They request the Kin

g to put an end to the deadly plague that is raging in the city The head priest tells him: and these children; not AS deeming thee 4 neN divinity but the first of men; First in the common accidents Of life, And first in visitations Of the Gods. The lines clearly state that the Greeks looked at the King as first among men and not as a God He is also the first to suffer the visitation of the Gods. Visitation ' here means a catastrophic event, seen as a punishment from God.

Sophocles: Oedipus Rex

Oedipus is variedly addressed as the peerless king the ship of the state chief of men and is requested to upraise our state Oedipus tells him that he had already sent Creon, his brother in-law (his wife s brother) to the Delphic Oracle to learn what he should do to put an end to the deadly plague. The Delphic Oracle was the famous Oracle of Apollo who gives cryptic predictions and guidance to both city states (states consisting of a sovereign cities) and individuals. Though its predictions were noted for their ambiguity it was held by the ancient Greeks to be of great authority He says that he will perform all that the god declares to Creon to be performed When Creon comes, he wonders whether he should speak to Oedipus in private.

Oedipus : How YunS the oracle? Thus far thy words Give me n० ground for confidence 0r fear

Creon: If thou wouldst hear my message publicly Ill tell thee straight, 0r with thee pass within.

Oedipus : Speak before all; the burden that _ Dear ls more for these my subjects than myself

Analysis: Oedipus sense of fairness and justice is evident as he refuses to have any secret meeting with Creon. He wants the message from the Oracle to be publicly heard. Creon comes in and tells him in the presence of all assembled as desired by Oedipus that his predecessor King Laius has been murdered long ago and and now the god ५ command is plain: Punish his takers-off, whoe'er they be ' Oedipus promises his citizens to find the murderer and punish him and restore the city from the deadly pestilence. The Chorus enters with prayers to Athena, daughter of Zeus to save the city from ruin and death. Oedipus decides to solve the murder of Laius. He first asks the citizens: N9l This proclamation [ address to all: Thebans, if any knows the mAn by whom Laius , SOn of Labdacus, WAS slain, Summon him t० make clean shrift t० me And if he shrinks, let him refiect that thus Confessing he shall scape the capital charge; For the 0rSt penalty that shall befall him Is banishment unscathed he shall depart But if an alien from 0 foreign land Be known t० an) AS the murderer, Let him who knows speak Out , ana ne shali have Due recompense from me He tells them of his resolve to punish the murderer but his statement is laden with irony as later he discovers that he is the murderer And 0n the murderer this CurSe L lay (On him and all the partners in his guilt): Wretch, may he pine in utter wretcheaness ! And for myself, if with my privity He gain aamittance t० my hearth, - pray The CurSe +L laid 0n others fall 0n me

Analysis: Oedipus is vehement in his resolve to punish Laius murderer even if that person happens to be close to him. It is important to note that at no point of time does Oedipus imagine that he is the murderer It is this confidence of personal probity that gets shattered at the end This is the tragic irony of the play His extreme belief in his inviolability is to be seen as his blind spot and this blindness to the possibility of complicity in the murder suggests an element of overweening pride that almost justifies his own blind spot. On the advice of the chorus, Oedipus summons the blind prophet Tiresias.

Oedipus Rex: Summary and Analysis
title: 2.2 SUMMARY AND ANALYSIS OF OEDIPUS REX: SECTION 1: LINES ] 337 The play begins with Oedipus meeting a procession of priests and citizens of his kingdom in Thebes. They request the Kin

text:
2.3 SUMMARY AND ANALYSIS OF OEDIPUS REX: SECTION 2: LINES ३३8 706

The prophet at first refuses to name the murderer, but sensing the wrath of Oedipus, he speaks the truth: Tiresias: Thox art the mar, Thou the accursed polluter of this land Analysis: Oedipus cannot believe what Tiresias has to say and asks him to say it again. Oedipus : but half caught thy meaning; say it again. Tiresias : [ say thou art the murderer of the man gnou Whose murderer thou pursuest. [ say thou livest with thy nearest kin In infamy, unwitting in thy shame. Analysis: Oedipus refuses to believe him and suspects Creon s hand in fabricating the falsehood. He orders the blind prophet to move out, but not OPLES before the latter hints at incestuous marriage and a future of blindness and infamy Tiresias: Say thou livest with thy nearest kin |NIVERSITY In infamy unwitting in thy shame. Hear then: this man whom thou hast sought to arrest With threats and warrants this long while, the wretch Who murdered Laius that man is here. He passes for An alien i७ the lana But S00n shall prove 0 Theban, native borr. And yet his fortune brings him littie joy; For blind of seeing clad in beggar 5 weeds, For purple robes, and leaning 0n his staf, To 0 strange land ne S00n shall grope his way Analysis: This reveals Oedipus ' strong resolve to arrest and punish the murderer of King Laius and the irony is that he happens to be the murderer The blind sccr correctly says that the murderer will initially be regarded as an alien and later will be proved to be a native Theban. Oedipus is in reality the Theban prince who had been born to Laius and Jocasta and who returns to Thebes a$ १ person from Corinth .

Tiresias : And of the children, inmates of his home, He shall be proved the brother and the sire, Of her who bare him S0n and husband both,

Sophocles: Oedipus Rex

Co-partner and assassin of his sire. Go in and ponder this, and if thou find That _ have missed the mark, henceforth declare have n० wit nor skill in prophecy

Analysis: Once again we cannot miss the dramatic irony. Tiresias is blind, but he can see the truth. He is strong in the sense of truth. Oedipus has sight, but he cannot see the truth. Oedipus taunts Tiresias saying thou in ear, wit, 2)e, in everything art blind. Then Oedipus charges Creon for conspiring with the Priest tell a falsehood. He cannot visualise that he could be the murderer

Oedipus : Question and prove me murderer if thow canst. Creon : Then let me ask thee, didst thow weQ my sister? Oedipus : A fact S० plain _ cannot well deny Creon : And AS thy consort queen she shares the throne? Oedipus : + grant her freely all her heart desires. Creon: And with )0% twain + share the triple rule? Oedipus : Yea, and it is that proves thee A false friend. Analysis: The Question answer dialogue affirms Oedipus is the murderer The Chorus warns Oedipus to respect man (Creon ) whose probity is known to all. Oedipus ' charge against Creon and Tiresias is another instance of his arrogance that he, the great Oedipus can never be at fault. This narcissistic arrogance as result of his self belief in his own greatness almost divine like is true to the saying ` pride goes before a fall . Having said that we would also need to remember that since Oedipus has no idea about his reall birth biological parents and because he has been raised in Corinth from the time he is a new born baby he sincerely believes that he could not have killed King Laius who is his real father
title: 2.3 SUMMARY AND ANALYSIS OF OEDIPUS REX: SECTION 2: LINES ३३8 706

text:
2.4 SUMMARY AND ANALYSIS OF OEDIPUS REX: SECTION 3: LINES ७०७-1००७

Incensed with both Creon and Tiresias, Oedipus turns to his wife Jocasta. She asks him to ignore these prophecies in the light of the Delphic Oracle that had earlier prophesied Laius death at the hands of their son who would eventually marry his mother But she believes that prophecy did not happen as they had abandoned their baby and were subsequently informed that he had died early So Jocasta argues that the son could not have killed his father nor married his mother Laius, according to her was killed by a band of robbers 0n १ crossroad. This is how Jocasta comforts her present husband,

Jocasta : Then thou mayest ९०Se thy conscience 0n that SCOre Listen and Ill convince thee that n० man Hath scot 0r lot in the prophetic art. Here is the proof in brief An oracle Once came to Laius ( will not SA) Twas from the Delphic god himself, but from His ministers) declaring he was doomed To perish by the hand of his 0wn 50n, 4 child that shouid be born t० him by me Now Laius S0 at least report afirmed

Was murdered 0n 0 day by highwaymen, No natives, at 0 spot where three roads meet. As for the child, it WAS but three days old, When Laius, its ankles pierced and pinned Together gave it t० be CaSt awa By others 0n the trackiess mountain side So then Apollo brought it n0t t० pass The child should be his father s murderer Or the dread terror find accomplishment, And Laius be slain by his 0wn $01. Such WAS the prophet $ horoscope. 0 king Regard it not Whate ' er the god deems fit To search, himself unaided will reveal.

Oedipus Rex: Summary and Analysis

The irony is that, Jocasta is not aware that her child abandoned and sent away to be killed lest it should commit the heinous crime of killing his father and marrying his mother is none other than Oedipus, who was saved and TaS brought up by the King and Queen of Corinth as their s0n
title: 2.4 SUMMARY AND ANALYSIS OF OEDIPUS REX: SECTION 3: LINES ७०७-1००७

text:
2.5 SUMMARY AND ANALYSIS OF OEDIPUS REX: SECTION ४: LINES १००8 1310 Oedipus had earlier heard the prophecy when he was in Corinth as the son OU of the King and Queen of Corinth. Oedipu

s has no clue about his adoption by the Corinth King and Queen. So he decides to run away from Corinth to avoid committing such heinous crime Now a$ King of Thebes, while waiting to solve the murder of Laius, he learns that the King of Corinth Polybus has died of natural causes and he feels relieved that the prophecy had not come OPLES true The Messenger from Corinth shocks him by disclosing the truth that the King and Queen of Corinth were not his genetic parents, but only his foster parents. The messenger says that a shepherd had rescued the abandoned baby RSITY of Laius and Jocasta and given it to him and he had handed the baby over to the King and the queen who were childless. It is at this point Oedipus recalls his fight at a crossroad when he was running away from Corinth with someone who resembled Laius . The story unfolds before his eyes. Desiring to get to the bottom of the truth, he resolves to track down the shepherd who had rescued the abandoned baby of Laixs and Jocasta and learn the truth about his birth He tells Jocasta Oedipus : Who has 0 higher claim that thou t० hear My tale of dire adventures? Listen then . My sire was Polybus of Corinth, and My mother Merope, A Dorian; Ana WAS held the foremost citizen, Till A strange thing befell me, strange indeed, Yet scarce deserving all the heat it stirred. 4 roisterer at Some banquet, flown with wine, Shouted "Thou art not true S0n of thy sire Analysisः This is how he believed himself to be the son of King Polybus and Queen Merope and it explains why he left Corinth in the first place till someone discloses that he was not their true son Sophocles: Oedipus Rex Oedipus : It irred me, but stomached for the nOnCe The insult; 0n the morrOw sought out My mother and my sire and questioned them. They were indignant at the random Sur Cast 0n my parentage and dia their best To comjort me, but still the venomea baro Rankled, for still the scandal spread and grew. So privily without their leave _ went To Delphi, and Apollo sent me back Baulked of the knowledge that came t० seer. But other grievous things ne prophesied, Analysis: This disclosure bothers him and to find the truth about his life he goes to Delphi to consult the Oracle. Though the Oracle does not give him the answer to his question, he prophesies that Oedipus will slay his father and marry his mother The Delphic Oracle had marked Oedipus twice-in Thebes and in Corinth as the future perpetrator of crime against his parents Woes, lamentations, mourning portents dire; To wit I should defile my mother s bed And raise up seed too loathsome to behold, What Gods decree, n० man cAn escape. And slay the father from whose loins sprang Then, lady thou shalt hear the U very truth As च drew near the triple-branching roads, A herald met me and A man who sat In A CAY drawn by colts AS in thy tale The man in front and the old man himself Threatened to thrust me rudely from the paाh LES Then jostled by the charioteer in wrath I struck him, and the old man, seeing this, Watched till [ passed and from his CAr brought dow[ Y Full 0n my head the double pointed goad. Yet WAS [ quits with him and more; On९ stroke Of m good staff sufficed t० fling him clean Out of the chariot seat and laid him prone And S० _ slew them ever ) 018. But if Betwixt this stranger there wAS aught in common With Laius, who more miserable than L . What mortal could you find more god-abhorred? Wretch whom n० sojourner, n० citizen May harbor 0r address, whom all are bouna To harry from their homes. And this same CurSe Was laia 0n me and laid by nOne but me Yea with these hands all gory pollute The bed of him I slew. Say am + vile? Am _ not utterly unclean, 0 wretch Doomed t० be banished, and in banishment Forgo the sight of all my dearest OnRS, And never tread again myy native earth ; Or else to wed my mother and slay my sire, Polybus, who begat me and upreared? If one should say this is the handiwork Of some inhuman power who could blame His judgment? But, ye pure and awful gods, Forbid, forbid that showld S९९ that day! May b९ blotted out from living men Ere such 0 plague spot set 0n me its brand! Oedipus Rex: Summary and Analysis Analysis: Oedipus takes the blame on himself though he knows
title: 2.5 SUMMARY AND ANALYSIS OF OEDIPUS REX: SECTION ४: LINES १००8 1310 Oedipus had earlier heard the prophecy when he was in Corinth as the son OU of the King and Queen of Corinth. Oedipu

text:
2.7 THE CHORUS

peIV In modern plays, the Chorus is rarely one of the dramatis personae. But in a classical tragedy the Chorus is an integral part of the play. Both the Chorus and the three Unities (which will be discussed in the next Unit (Unit 3) require each other to perform their respective functions. The ancient view of the Chorus is stated by Horace, the Latin poet (६५ BC 8 BCJ in his Ars of Poetry, Ars Poetica: Tne Chorus must back the good and give sage counsel; must control the passionate and cherish those that fear to do evil; it must praise the thrifty meal, the blessings of Justice, the Laws and Peace with her unbarred gates It will respect confidences and implore heaven that prosperity may revisit the miserable and quit the proud Horace 0n the Art of Poetry e०. by Edward Henry Blakeney 1928 The basic functions of the Chorus, as laid out by Horace gives the Chorus the role of an objective viewer of the events that unfold before their eyes. The Chorus is the upholder of virtue, morality and wisdom and underlines the moral of the play that implores prosperity to the unfortunate victims and woe to the proud and the haughty In other words, the Chorus speaks the voice of the audience, expressing its fears, anxieties, premonitions and wisdom They arrive early on in the play and they remain till the end In Oedipus Rex it will be noticed that they seem to know in advance the coming of disaster before Oedipus

knows it. They conclude with the message of the play about the appalling reversal of fortune that shows the power of the Gods over men. The summary of Oedipus Rex as given above in 5 sections, does not include the speeches of the Chorus. We will highlight those speeches and see how the Chorus becomes an integral part of the story as stated by Horace.

Oedipus Rex: Summary and Analysis
title: 2.7 THE CHORUS

text:
2.8 ANALYSIS OF THE SPEECHES OF THE CHORUS

The Chorus makes its first entry in Section with special prayers to the Gods to rid Thebes of the raging plague. Their prayers are answered by Oedipus who enters announcing that he will answer their prayers. The Chorus informs the audience that Phoebus Apollo, the god of the sun will give the answer, but Oedipus takes upon himself the role of a god १ role the Chorus has been reluctant to allow him. Oedipus is proudly conscious of his competence to find the murderer of Laius. He saysः no living man CAn hope to force the gods to speak against their will and therefore he will speak. Here he comes close to dismissing the gods and giving himself the right to answer the chorus although this does not amount to blasphemy His dialogue with the Chorus enables us to see early in the play Oedipus s dangerous pride in his inviolable character, almost displaying a willful blindness to any blemish in his character and this to a certain extent justifies his downfall. The Chorus is almost a crystal OU gazer who believes in divine sources for seeing the future. In Section 2 the Chorus tries to calm Oedipus down who in his arrogance charges both Creon and Tiresias of conspiring to overthrow him. It asks him not to be stubborn and unrelenting but TH[ pIOPLES respect A man whose probity and truth are known to all and nOW confirmed by oath. The Chorus thus gives the King sage counsel. The Chorus after hearing Tiresias RSITY is perplexed about believing and not believing his words The Chorus: Sore perplexed am by the words of the master $21. Are the ) true, are the ) false? L know not and bridle my tongue for fear, Fluttered with vague surmise; nOr present n0r Juture is clear Proof is there noneः how then can ] challenge OuY King $ good name, How i७ 0 blood feud join for An untracked deed of shame? All wise are Zeus and Apollo, and nothing is hid from their ken; They Are gods; and in wits 0 man may surpass his fellow men ; But that 0 mortal Se९r Knows more than + Know where Hath this been proven ? Or how without sign assured, CAn blame Him who saved OuY State when the winged songstress came, Tested and tried in the light of us all, like gold assayed? How Can nOW assent when 0 crime is 0n Oedipus laid? Analysis: The Chorus like all other men cannot think of Oedipus as the murderer When there is no proof, it is not possible to challenge the King s good name, in which case it would also be difficult to believe the blind seer? The Chorus is terrified, helpless, confused and full of anxiety and uncertainty After Oedipus and Jocasta leave the stage, the Chorus enters, announcing that the world is ruled by destiny and denouncing arrogant men who defy the gods. 21 Sophocles: Oedipus Rex Throughout the play the Chorus is miserable, and desperate for the plague to end and for stability to be restored to the city Nevertheless, the Chorus holds staunchly to the belief that the prophecies of Tiresias will come true For if they do not, there is no order on earth or in the heavens The Chorus is the voice of the Greek faith in decrees made by God. The only way to restore order both in heaven and on earth is not to override the decree of the divine powers. The Chorus makes its appearance again in the final section. The Chorus laments that even Oedipus, the greatest of men, has been brought low by destiny for an unknowing crime committed out of good intentions. Oedipus ' crimes are presented as kind of blight on the land १ plague symbolised by the plague with which the play begins that infects the earth on which Oedipus, his family and his citizens stand, and in which all have been victimised as a result of Oedipus s violent acts The Chorus sums up the fate of man in its speeches in the last section The Chorus: Of insolence is bred The tyrant; insolence full blown, With empty riches surfeited, Scales the precipitous height and grasps the Then topples o'er and lies in ruin गl Oedipus : Curse On the mAn who took the cruel bonds from off my legs , AS [ lay in the field. He stole me Jrom death and saved me, n० kindly service. Had [ died then THE pEOPLES L would not be S0 burdensome to friends Analysis: The Chorus charges Oedipus with tyranny born of insolence Oedipus ascent to the throne and his conscious awareness of his kingly qualities of probity fairness and justice make him assume almost tyrannical against sinners-
title: 2.8 ANALYSIS OF THE SPEECHES OF THE CHORUS

text:
2.9 LET US SUM UP

In this unit we have summarised the play into five sections and also analysed the play to an extent. Having gone through the play we have seen the rise, the heights that Oedipus rose to and his subsequent fall. We saw how the Gods can snatch away what they ve given in the wink of an eye and we saw Oedipus reduced to an extremely troubled, almost deranged, blind beggarly state from being King of Thebes . We also learnt a little bit about hamartial tragic flaw and finally we examined the role, the purpose and significance of the Chorus in Classical Greek Tragedies. In the next unit we will take up for discussion Aristotle s three unities Unity of Time, Unity of Place and Unity of Action.
title: 2.9 LET US SUM UP

text:
